item.content:encoded: Congrats on the +100! This skip really deserves it, I'm a sucker for heartwarming skips. Of course I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too early for Caturday, but… simple, sweet, and a great reflection of pets, with an interesting backstory and dash of mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After binge-reading a lot of articles it's nice to see one that doesn't involve the wholesale slaughter of D-Class personnel or ancient utterings of dark and unnerving deities.

This is just a strange, shared hallucination (possibly) which generally leaves people the better for having experienced it, and that's delightful.

It's also nice to see a slightly warmer side to the Foundation - the writing and distribution of a children's book about Ubaste to create a cover story instead of widespread use of amnestics is a lovely touch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A magic cat that improves people's lives through the power of friendship?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm. I like the general effect overall, especially the bit about exploration, but the interview sapped it all out for me. It took it from "Mysterious stray cat enters people's lives or memories and everyone is the better for it" in kind of a vague yet interesting way all the way up to "This is some "Saturday morning cartoon'-level schlock and everyone involved is perfectly aware of that fact."

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's nice to see a heartwarming skip every now and then.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. There are a few tonal issues around the "Discovery" section.

2. As a communist, the interviewee being named "Marx" kinda threw me off but…

UPVOTE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know… I think that cat is actually a… FAIRY GOD PARENT!

;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice, uplifting article, good work for a first skip. :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fantastically unique recovery log salvages what is otherwise a rather bland article.

item.title: A few notes
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2643 was discovered after a security exploit within the ████ ██████ College's database access systems, resulting in a public dumping of authorization questions for the recovery of lost accounts.

Better as "…was discovered after a security exploit within… database access systems resulted in a public dumping…"

Overall it's effective and good. It successfully does what is intended. I'd suggest in the future to pay attention to sharpnesses in prose (this scp doesn't have many, which is to its benefit, but any future articles the author may want to write with more punch will suffer without it, and a piece can always be improved with tighter language).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(ﾉ◕ヮ◕)ﾉ*:・ﾟ✧*:・ﾟ✧*:・ﾟ✧

I'm happy to see this on the site!!

I'm a sucker for uplifting skips, and this is nicely written. Good job.

Is good to see anomalies with good intentions every now and then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's cool. If I managed to get my very first SCP universally liked I would have been surprised. I guess there's nothing I could do to make you like it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoting for a couple of (albeit personal) reasons:

  • The effect wasn't that interesting to me. It was well written, it's just a personal point.
  • I didn't feel anything from the article personally, and I get it's supposed to be a light hearted article.

Well written, I'll give you that, I just didn't like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OK, I'll accept that. Changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll second that- using Ubaste would probably be better. Good article though. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this! It does people good in the most inconspicuous manner possible, for reasons that are at best obscure.

To the aspiring contributors reading this - a SCP does not have to be a monstrous whatsit that murderdeathkills people. This one definitely demonstrates that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Ubaste." "Your cat." "The cat." "Der kittonen." "Kisse." "Da widdle fwuffykins." "Nanami."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, on the meta level, I couldn't think of any way to phrase the questions that didn't sound even more awkward.

In-universe, they have a very simple and inconspicuous explanation of the term, which is explained in detail in the part of the interview which is not transcribed in the article.

But if it really throws people off, I'll gladly change it if anyone has got a suggestion of a better way to write it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great but I have to nitpick one detail:

Why the hell does the interviewer say "SCP-2643?" If I weren't aware of this site and people started talking about SCP-this-or-that, I'd have a TON of questions, questions the Foundation wouldn't want people asking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually really like this. It's unique, creative, and I really like the theory that it's an actual psychic entity.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first!

Big thanks to Zyn and SoullessSingularity for helping a new guy out!
